title: Listening - Part 4: Keeping Bees in the City
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a man called Ahmed, who is an urban beekeeper. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: Today’s guest is Ahmed Khan, who keeps bees on the roofs of city buildings. Ahmed, how did that happen? || Ahmed: Completely by accident. I was working in an office with a flat roof, and a local environmental group asked whether they could place two hives there. I offered to help one weekend. [24]I became interested when I realised how many different flowering plants bees could find even in the middle of a city[/24]. Soon I was doing a training course and looking after hives myself. || Presenter: Do people worry about bees being close to workplaces and homes? || Ahmed: At first, yes. People imagine clouds of angry insects near their windows. In fact, honeybees usually ignore people if treated sensibly. [25]Before installing any hives, I hold a meeting so neighbours can ask questions and understand what will happen[/25]. Once they have accurate information, most are pleased to support the project. || Presenter: Is city honey different from country honey? || Ahmed: It can be. Rural bees may feed mainly on a single crop for a period. City bees encounter garden flowers, park trees and plants on balconies. [26]Because they collect nectar from such a wide mixture of plants, urban hives often produce honey with a surprisingly complex flavour[/26]. Each part of the city tastes slightly different. || Presenter: Do roofs make ideal places for hives? || Ahmed: They are safe from people passing close by and usually get good light. But there is a disadvantage that I underestimated at the start. [27]In summer a roof can become extremely hot, so I must make sure the bees have shade and a reliable supply of water[/27]. It’s something every rooftop beekeeper needs to plan for. || Presenter: Are bees difficult to care for during winter? || Ahmed: You don’t open the hive frequently in cold weather because that would disturb them. The key work actually happens earlier. [28]At the end of autumn, I check that each colony has stored enough food to survive until flowers appear again[/28]. Weak colonies may need additional food before winter begins. || Presenter: What is most satisfying about the work? || Ahmed: Selling honey helps pay the costs, but it is not the best part. Some of our hives are on school roofs. [29]When children who were initially afraid begin asking intelligent questions about pollination, I feel the project has achieved something important[/29]. Bees make environmental issues real for them. || Presenter: Should listeners try keeping their own bees? || Ahmed: Not immediately. They are living creatures, and mistakes can harm a whole colony. [30]I advise anyone interested to assist an experienced beekeeper through a full season before buying equipment[/30]. That way they see both the enjoyable days and the difficult ones.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What first attracted Ahmed to urban beekeeping?

options.A: The possibility of making honey at work.
options.B: The variety of plants available to city bees.
options.C: A desire to join an environmental organisation.

questions.explanation: Ahmed became interested when he saw how many flowering plants bees could find in the city.

questions.question: How does Ahmed deal with concerns about new hives?

options.A: He discusses the project with local people beforehand.
options.B: He places the hives where nobody can see them.
options.C: He asks an environmental group to answer complaints.

questions.explanation: He meets neighbours before installing hives so they can ask questions.

questions.question: What does Ahmed say about honey produced in the city?

options.A: It is normally sweeter than rural honey.
options.B: Its taste may be interesting because bees visit many plants.
options.C: Its quality depends mainly on balcony flowers.

questions.explanation: A wide mixture of nectar sources often gives city honey a complex flavour.

questions.question: What problem can rooftop hives have?

options.B: They are difficult to reach with equipment.
options.C: They can get too warm in hot weather.

questions.explanation: Ahmed says roofs may become extremely hot, requiring shade and water for the bees.

questions.question: What does Ahmed do before winter?

options.A: He makes sure the bees have enough food stored.
options.B: He moves each hive to a warmer place.
options.C: He opens the hives every week to check them.

questions.explanation: He checks in autumn that each colony has enough stored food to survive winter.

questions.question: What does Ahmed find most rewarding?

options.A: earning enough from honey to expand the project
options.B: encouraging children to understand bees better
options.C: showing that city honey tastes different

questions.explanation: Ahmed is pleased when previously afraid children become interested in pollination.

questions.question: What does Ahmed recommend to people interested in keeping bees?

options.A: Take responsibility for a small hive immediately.
options.B: Buy protective clothing before taking a course.
options.C: Gain practical experience with an expert first.

questions.explanation: He recommends helping an experienced beekeeper for a full season before buying equipment.
